claim_chain: Regime shifts are challenging to detect due to noisy signals and environmental volatility
Humans show systematic overreaction when signals are noisy but environments are stable
Underreaction occurs when signals are precise but environments are unstable
System neglect framework explains these biases through sensitivity to system parameters
Two distinct brain networks implement regime-shift detection with different computational roles
vmPFC represents sensitivity to transition probability while frontoparietal network represents sensitivity to signal diagnosticity

typical_pattern: Opening statement of main finding or research question
Description of methodological approach or experimental design
Presentation of key results with specific details
Interpretation linking results to computational framework
Comparison with related work or alternative paradigms
Implications for broader understanding of the phenomenon

likely_priorities: Distinction from reversal-learning paradigm and justification of novel task design
Dissociation of change detection from reward processing mechanisms
Clarity on whether change detection is independent of reinforcement learning
Computational modeling validation and parameter recovery
Neural network specificity and functional dissociation
likely_attacks: Task design differences from standard reversal-learning paradigm may limit comparability
Explicit instruction about regimes removes ecological validity of learning through experience
Lack of reward contingency may not reflect real-world regime-shift scenarios
Generalizability beyond stylized laboratory task to real-world decision making
Whether neural findings are specific to this task or generalizable to change detection broadly
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The discussion extensively defends the task design by contrasting it with reversal-learning paradigms, emphasizing three major differences and explaining how these differences led to key insights. The authors explicitly address whether neural systems are contingent on reward involvement and whether change detection is dissociable from reinforcement learning. The phrase 'it has been challenging to infer' and 'it would be challenging to infer' signals anticipated reviewer concerns. The authors also emphasize that 'participants in our task did not have to learn about the different regimes through experience' and 'Without the confound of reinforcement learning' suggesting they anticipate criticism about ecological validity and learning mechanisms.

examples.quoted_phrase: we discovered that sensitivity to environmental parameters that directly impact change detection is a key mechanism for under- and overreactions
these findings suggest that selectivity and sensitivity of neural responses to system parameters are key mechanisms that give rise to under- and overreactions
might be part of a common pathway for change detection in general
our results help clarify the roles of change detection on choice behavior by suggesting that independent of learning, there exists a specialized change-detection mechanism in the brain

phrase_evidence.phrase: In this study, we investigated how humans detect changes in the environments and the neural mechanisms that contribute to how we might under- and overreact in our judgments
Combining a novel behavioral paradigm with computational modeling and fMRI
we discovered that sensitivity to environmental parameters that directly impact change detection is a key mechanism
By introducing the framework in system neglect and providing evidence for its neural implementations, this study offered both theoretical and empirical insights
Regime shifts—the transition from one state of the world to another—are present in many daily situations
Detecting regime shifts can be challenging for at least two reasons
These results suggest system neglect
At the neurobiological level, we found that regime-shift detection is jointly implemented by two networks
these findings suggest that selectivity and sensitivity of neural responses to system parameters are key mechanisms that give rise to under- and overreactions
Our work is closely related to the reversal-learning paradigm
there are three major differences between the reversal-learning paradigm and our regime-shift task
We believe that these major differences in task design led to three key insights
we were able to identify situations that led to over- and underreactions
we were able to provide a systematic account for these over- and underreactions in the system-neglect hypothesis
we were able to clarify whether the neural systems involved in change detection in the reversal-learning tasks are contingent on whether rewards are involved
it would be challenging to infer whether the neural implementations of change detection are dissociable from reward processing
Our findings that vmPFC and ventral striatum represent probability estimates of change and belief revision therefore suggest that these brain regions might be part of a common pathway
it has been challenging to infer whether there exists a unique change-detection mechanism that is dissociable from reinforcement learning mechanisms
Without the confound of reinforcement learning, our results help clarify the roles of change detection on choice behavior
there exists a specialized change-detection mechanism in the brain that impacts decision making
The world constantly changes, with the underlying state of the world shifting from one regime to another
determining whether a regime has changed is usually not obvious, as signals are noisy and reflective of the volatility of the environment
Human participants showed systematic overreaction and underreaction
These behavioral signatures are consistent with the system-neglect computational hypothesis
Guided by this computational framework, we found that individual subjects' sensitivity to system parameters was represented by two distinct brain networks
these results suggest that regime-shift detection recruits belief-updating and evidence-evaluation networks
under- and overreactions arise from how sensitive these networks are to the system parameters
In each trial, the subjects saw a sequence of red and/or blue signals and were told that these signals were drawn from one of the two regimes
Probability estimates from all subjects are plotted as histograms separately for each condition
We simulated probability estimates according to the system-neglect model

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, invented numbers, or author names were included. The profile is based entirely on the provided abstract, discussion, and figure legends.

_phrase_violations.phrase: Guided by this computational framework, we found that individual subjects' sensitivity to system parameters was represented by two distinct brain networks
Probability estimates from all subjects are plotted as histograms separately for each condition
